title: Performance and Optimization
summary: Profile TypeScript code, identify bottlenecks, and apply optimization techniques without sacrificing readability

sections.title: Performance Is About Measuring, Not Guessing
sections.content: Never guess what is slow — measure first. Human intuition about performance bottlenecks is notoriously wrong, like guessing which part of your commute takes the longest versus actually timing each segment. Use profiling tools: Node.js's built-in '--prof' flag, Chrome DevTools Performance tab, or the 'perf_hooks' module for precise timing. TypeScript itself adds zero runtime performance cost — all type information is erased during compilation. The performance characteristics of your code are identical to the equivalent JavaScript. Bottlenecks typically fall into three categories: algorithmic (wrong data structure), synchronous (blocking the event loop), or memory (excessive GC pressure). Fix the right one.

sections.title: Profiling and Optimizing a Slow Data Processing Function
sections.content: Compares O(n²) nested loops with an O(n) Map-based approach for finding duplicates

sections.title: Bundle Size, Tree Shaking, and Dead Code Elimination
sections.content: For frontend TypeScript, performance also means bundle size — how much JavaScript the browser must download. Tree shaking is a build process that removes unused exports, like pruning dead branches from a tree. To benefit, use named exports (not default exports), import only what you need ('import { map } from "lodash"' instead of 'import _ from "lodash"'), and configure your bundler (Vite, webpack, esbuild) for production mode. TypeScript's 'isolatedModules' and 'verbatimModuleSyntax' options help ensure your imports are clean. Also consider lazy loading: dynamically import() heavy modules only when needed, like loading the checkout page code only when the user clicks 'checkout'.

sections.title: Premature Optimization Adding Complexity Without Data
sections.content: The #1 advanced mistake is spending hours optimizing code that runs once per day or handles negligible data volumes. Wrong approach: 'I will cache this function result because it MIGHT be called frequently' without measuring. Correct: write clean, correct code first. Profile to find the actual bottleneck. Optimize only the top 3 hot spots. This follows the 80/20 rule: 80% of execution time is spent in 20% of the code. Donald Knuth famously said 'premature optimization is the root of all evil.' This does not mean 'write slow code' — it means 'write sensible code first, then optimize what matters.' An hour of profiling can save days of optimizing the wrong thing.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A log processing system has a function that reads an array of log entries (each with a timestamp string) and counts how many entries fall into each hour of the day. The current implementation uses nested loops and string parsing. Profile it with a simulated 100,000 entries (generate them with a loop). Identify the bottleneck and rewrite it to be at least 10x faster. Measure both versions with console.time. The function signature is: 'countByHour(entries: { timestamp: string }[]): Record<number, number>' returning hour -> count.
hints: What is the cost of parsing a timestamp string — can you parse it once per entry instead of multiple times?
What data structure gives O(1) lookup for accumulating counts by hour?
What type of loop is fastest in JavaScript/TypeScript — for-of, forEach, or a traditional for loop?
